name: Left Posterior Fascicular Block
aliases: Right Axis Deviation due to LPFB
identifyBy: Right axis deviation (axis +90° to +180°) — must exclude other causes
Small R, deep S in I, aVL (rS pattern lateral)
Small Q, tall R in II, III, aVF (qR pattern inferior)
Diagnosis of exclusion: no RV hypertrophy, no lateral MI, no normal variant (young, thin)
management: LPFB alone: no treatment required — rare in isolation
Uncommon: posterior fascicle has dual blood supply (LAD + RCA)
LPFB occurrence strongly suggests significant coronary artery disease
Cardiology referral — risk of bifascicular block and progression
RBBB + LPFB = bifascicular block with high risk of complete heart block
pearls: LPFB is much rarer than LAFB — posterior fascicle has dual blood supply (harder to block)
Always exclude right heart strain, RV hypertrophy, and lateral MI before diagnosing LPFB
LPFB with anterior MI = suggests large infarct involving both fascicles — poor prognosis
Right axis deviation mnemonic: Remember LPFB is the rare one — isolated right axis is usually normal variant in young patients
differentials: RV hypertrophy (right axis, tall R V1, other features)
Lateral MI (Q waves in I, aVL)
Normal variant right axis (young, thin patients)
